explanation: SET NOCOUNT OFF leaves row-count messages enabled, increasing client and network overhead.
SET NOCOUNT OFF changes a session setting and is not restored before the procedure ends.
SET XACT_ABORT OFF changes a session setting and is not restored before the procedure ends.
SET XACT_ABORT OFF allows some run-time errors to leave a transaction partially applied.
SELECT * on Sales.Orders couples callers to every current and future column.
SELECT TOP (10) on Sales.Orders has no ORDER BY, so the returned rows are nondeterministic.
DISTINCT on Sales.Orders.OrderID is unnecessary because OrderID is the primary key.
WITH (NOLOCK) on Sales.Orders can return uncommitted, missing, or duplicated rows.
YEAR(o.OrderDate) in the predicate prevents a direct range seek on OrderDate.
Year value 2016 is embedded directly in the predicate instead of being parameterized.
Comparing Sales.Orders.CustomerID (int) with @CustomerIdText (nvarchar) forces an implicit conversion.
OR-based optional filter @CustomerID IS NULL OR o.CustomerID = @CustomerID harms cardinality estimation and seeking.
LIKE pattern N'%urgent%' on Sales.Orders.Comments starts with a leading wildcard, preventing an index seek.
NOT IN against a subquery on i.CustomerID yields incorrect results if the subquery ever produces NULL.
ISNULL(o.CustomerID, 0) in the predicate wraps the column and makes the filter non-sargable.
Arithmetic o.OrderID + 0 on the primary key column prevents direct index matching.
Business identifier 1 is hard-coded in the CustomerID predicate.
Inequality o.CustomerID <> 1 on Sales.Orders often scans broad portions of the table.
CROSS JOIN between Sales.Orders and Sales.Customers multiplies rows with no join predicate.
SELECT TOP (1) on the cross-joined result has no ORDER BY, so the returned row is nondeterministic.
Comma-separated FROM clause with join predicate in WHERE is old-style syntax and error-prone.
SELECT TOP (1) on the old-style join has no ORDER BY, so the returned row is nondeterministic.
Correlated scalar COUNT(*) subquery against Sales.OrderLines runs once per Sales.Orders row.
Correlated EXISTS against Sales.OrderLines could be expressed as a set join instead of a per-row lookup.
The CASE expression on Sales.Orders.Comments is repeated in both the SELECT list and ORDER BY clause.
ORDER BY 2 references a select-list position and breaks if the column list changes.
OFFSET/FETCH ordered only by Sales.Orders.OrderDate lacks a stable tie-breaker.
OrderID literals 1 and 2 are embedded directly in the query branches.
UNION combines disjoint branches (OrderID = 1 and OrderID = 2), causing unnecessary duplicate removal.
Correlated scalar COUNT(*) subquery against Sales.OrderLines runs once per Sales.Orders row.
COUNT(*) > 0 from Sales.OrderLines counts all matching rows when only existence is needed.
SELECT @OrderID = o.OrderID FROM Sales.Orders silently accepts multiple rows and keeps the last value.
Table variable @OrderIds may receive poor cardinality estimates on older compatibility levels.
SELECT TOP (5) inserted into @OrderIds has no ORDER BY, so the chosen rows are nondeterministic.
SELECT ... INTO #ImplicitOrders from Sales.Orders creates the temp table schema implicitly.
SELECT TOP (5) into #ImplicitOrders has no ORDER BY, so the chosen rows are nondeterministic.
Cursor order_cursor over Sales.Orders processes rows one at a time instead of set-wise.
SELECT TOP (2) feeding the cursor has no ORDER BY, so the chosen rows are nondeterministic.
WHILE loop increments @Counter iteratively rather than using a set-based approach.
SQL string is built by concatenating @CustomerID directly into the query text.
User-supplied parameter @CustomerID is concatenated into a dynamic SQL string, creating a SQL injection risk.
EXEC (@Sql) executes dynamic SQL without parameterized inputs.
SQL string is built by concatenating @OrderID directly into the query text.
User-supplied parameter @OrderID is concatenated into a dynamic SQL string, creating a SQL injection risk.
sp_executesql is used but the statement is concatenated rather than parameterized.
Explicitly created temp table #Scratch is not dropped before the procedure ends.
CATCH block selects ERROR_MESSAGE() but neither rethrows with THROW nor returns a failure status.
RAISERROR is used instead of THROW for the new error path.
PRINT is buffered and unsuitable for structured diagnostics.
Hard-coded CustomerID value 1 is embedded in the query instead of being parameterized or looked up.
Unlabelled date constant '20160101' is embedded in the Sales.Orders filter logic.
CONVERT(date, '01/02/2016') relies on session-sensitive date string interpretation.
COALESCE in the join predicate between Sales.Orders and Sales.Invoices inhibits normal join optimization.
SELECT TOP (1) on the COALESCE join has no ORDER BY, so the returned row is nondeterministic.
Self-join on Sales.Orders only retrieves OrderID, which is already available in the same row.
SELECT TOP (1) on the self-join has no ORDER BY, so the returned row is nondeterministic.
ORDER BY Sales.Orders.Comments (nvarchar(MAX)) performs a wide, unselective sort.
SUM(Quantity) over the entire Sales.OrderLines table aggregates a large fact-like table without a restrictive filter.
Row-level predicate ol.OrderID > 0 on Sales.OrderLines is placed in HAVING instead of WHERE.
ORDER BY OrderCount is redundant for a single-row aggregate result from Sales.Orders.
MERGE on Sales.Orders has documented concurrency and correctness edge cases.
MERGE UPDATE sets target.Comments = target.Comments, producing writes/logging with no data change.
UPDATE Sales.Orders SET Comments = Comments changes no data but still causes logging and locking.
DELETE from Sales.OrderLines uses the non-key column Quantity = -1 rather than a stable key predicate.
Hard-coded sentinel value -1 is embedded in the Sales.OrderLines delete predicate.
WAITFOR DELAY blocks a worker instead of using asynchronous scheduling.
BEGIN TRANSACTION is not wrapped in a BEGIN TRY / BEGIN CATCH error-handling boundary.
SET IDENTITY_INSERT ON on Sales.Orders changes session state and introduces fragile identity-management coupling.
System procedure sp_who is invoked without a schema qualifier.
SET ROWCOUNT 1 changes a session setting and is not restored before the procedure ends.
Hard-coded CategoryID value 99 is embedded in the INSERT into #DemoCategories.
INSERT INTO #DemoCategories VALUES omits the explicit column list.
LIKE pattern '%' + @CustomerName + '%' on Sales.Customers.CustomerName starts with a leading wildcard.
SQL string is built by concatenating @CustomerName directly into the LIKE pattern.
The constructed dynamic query uses a LIKE pattern that starts with a leading wildcard.
User-supplied parameter @CustomerName is concatenated into a dynamic SQL string, creating a SQL injection risk.
sp_executesql is used but the statement is concatenated rather than parameterized.
System procedure sp_executesql is invoked without a schema qualifier.
Hard-coded CustomerCategoryID value 1 is embedded in the UPDATE of #DemoCustomers.
UPDATE #DemoCustomers lacks a WHERE clause and would change every row.
Unlabelled date constant '2016-01-01' is embedded in the Sales.Orders filter logic.
Predicate on the outer-joined Sales.Orders table is placed in the WHERE clause, converting the LEFT JOIN to an inner join.
Sales.Customers.DeliveryMethodID = NULL uses equality with NULL instead of IS NULL and will never match.
Scalar user-defined function dbo.GetCustomerType in the SELECT list forces row-by-row evaluation.
CATCH block selects ERROR_MESSAGE() but neither rethrows with THROW nor returns a failure status.
CATCH block inside an active transaction does not roll back before handling the error.
Hard-coded index hint WITH (INDEX = 1) on Sales.Customers should be avoided in production code.
Subquery is nested three levels deep and should be refactored to CTEs or temp tables.
LIKE pattern '%A%' on Sales.Customers.CustomerName starts with a leading wildcard.
Multi-statement table-valued function dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti is used where an inline TVF would optimize better.
SELECT * from dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti couples callers to every column returned by the TVF.
